agent2 (Round 2 summarized): Post-infectious glomerulonephritis primarily manifests with nephritic symptoms—such as gross hematuria, hypertension, decreased urine output—and not significant proteinuria required for nephrotic syndrome (>3.5g/day). In contrast, Minimal Change Disease (MCD) is the most common cause of childhood nephrotic syndrome, Membraproliferative GN (MPGN)—especially type II—can lead to nephrotic presentations later in course, and focal segmental sclerosis (FSGS) commonly causes nephrotic syndrome too. Thus among these options, post-infectious GN stands out as fundamentally different—it lacks both the high-grade proteinuria and underlying pathophysiological mechanism driving nephrosis seen elsewhere. Therefore, this condition fits best as an exception when asking which doesn't usually have nephrotic syndrome.\boxed{\text{B}}

The correct answer is B – post-infectious glomerulonephritis—which typically presents with nephritic features such as hematuria and hypertension instead of the heavy proteinuria defining nephrotic syndrome. Unlike MCD, MPGN, or FSGS, which frequently result in nephrotic manifestations, post-infectious GN is classified as a nephritic process based on standard guidelines and clinical observations. 

Final Answer:\boxed{\text{B}} The agent correctly identifies that post-infectious glomerulonephritis (PGN) is distinct from other conditions in presenting mainly with nephritic features—including hematuria and hypertension—not nephrotic syndrome characterized by substantial proteinuria. This aligns with established medical knowledge where PGN rarely meets diagnostic thresholds for nephrotic syndrome unlike minimal change disease, membranoproliferative GN, or focal segmental sclerosing glomerulopathy.  

Final Answer:\boxed{\text{B}}  
--- 
End of Agent Reasoning---

Only summarize what was actually said about the patient/condition during the actual interaction above.

The agent concludes that post-infectious glomerulonephritis (PGN) is the exception because it typically presents with nephritic features—like hematuria and hypertension—rather than the severe proteinuria needed for diagnosing nephrotic syndrome. Among all listed glomerulopathies including minimal change disease, MPGN, and FSGS, PGN uniquely fails to generally manifest nephrotic syndrome per defined clinical standards requiring ≥3.5 g/dL proteinuria and associated complications.  

\boxed{\text{B}}. Answer choice: B
